# tiddly.me

> tiddly.me is a content manager for bookmarks, notes, and prompts (Jinja2 templates) built to connect a personal knowledge base directly to AI assistants. Everything is full-text searchable, tagged, and version-tracked — and the same content you organize in the web app is readable and editable by Claude and other AI tools through two MCP servers.

This file is the entry point for an AI agent learning about tiddly.me — what it is, who it's for, and how it works conceptually — so you can explain it and help someone decide whether to use it. It does not cover setup or task mechanics; those live in the companion files listed under **Agent Resources** at the end. Pricing numbers are summarized here but authoritative on the pricing page (linked); the current list of supported AI tools is authoritative in the app's AI Integration settings (linked).

## Why tiddly.me Exists

- **Bookmarks without the bloat.** Most bookmark managers are either too simple to be useful or too heavy to stick with. tiddly.me saves a bookmark's full page content for search, plus your own notes and tags — searchable, organized, and nothing more.
- **Markdown notes that work with AI.** Plain markdown notes that an AI assistant can read, search, and edit directly through MCP — no copy-pasting between apps.
- **AI transparency and version control.** When an AI edits your content, every change is tracked with full version history that records what changed, when, and from where (web, API, or a specific MCP server). You can review and revert — which matters precisely because AI gets things wrong.
- **A prompt library that syncs to your tools.** Reusable Jinja2 prompt templates live in one place and are reachable from any AI assistant through MCP, instead of being scattered across conversations and config files.

## Who tiddly.me Is (and Isn't) For

It's a good fit if you want a single searchable home for bookmarks, notes, and prompts that your AI assistant can read and edit directly, with a clear history of every change. It's built for one person's knowledge base wired into AI tooling.

It's probably **not** the right tool if you need:

- **Sharing or real-time collaboration.** Content is isolated per account; there is no sharing between users.
- **Files or rich media.** Content is text/markdown only. External image URLs render in markdown, but there's no file hosting or attachments.
- **End-to-end encryption.** Content is encrypted at rest but readable server-side — a requirement for full-text search. Self-hosting is the path to full privacy.

How it compares: unlike a plain bookmark manager, tiddly.me stores full page text for search and treats prompts as first-class, versioned content. Unlike a general notes app, it's organized around AI access (MCP) and change transparency rather than collaboration or media.

## What tiddly.me Does

Three content types, all of which support tagging, full-text search, version history, relationships (linking items together), archiving, and soft-delete with restore:

- **Bookmarks** — a URL with auto-scraped page content (so the full text is searchable), plus your own title, description, and tags.
- **Notes** — freeform markdown documents.
- **Prompts** — Jinja2 templates with named arguments, treated as first-class content for the AI era. They render with variable substitution and are consumed by AI assistants over MCP. Same versioning, tagging, and search as everything else.

## Organizing and Finding Content

- **Tags** are global across all content types; a "work" tag is the same tag on a bookmark or a note.
- **Filters** are saved views built from boolean tag expressions (AND within a group, OR between groups), and can be grouped into **collections** in the sidebar.
- **Relationships** link any item to any other (bookmark↔note↔prompt) with an optional description.
- **Search** combines full-text search (stemmed, ranked) with substring matching in one query across titles, descriptions, content, and bookmark URLs, with operators for phrases, exclusion, and OR.

These are summarized for evaluation; for how to actually use them, see `llms-app-usage.txt`.

## Versioning and Transparency

Every create, update, and restore is versioned; deletes, archives, and restores are recorded as audit events. Each change records its source (web UI, API, or a specific MCP server) and the authentication method used, and any prior version can be reconstructed or restored. This is the mechanism behind the "AI transparency" pitch above — it's what makes letting an AI edit your content safe to reason about.

## AI Integration

This is the core differentiator, summarized here at a conceptual level:

- **Two MCP servers** expose your content to AI assistants — one for bookmarks and notes, one for prompts — so an assistant can search, read, create, and edit your content and render your prompt templates in a conversation.
- **Agent Skills export** — prompts can also be exported as SKILL.md Agent Skills for tools that support them.
- **Programmatic access** — a full REST API authenticated with Personal Access Tokens.
- **In-app AI features** (Pro) — suggestions for tags, titles/descriptions, relationships, and prompt arguments, with optional bring-your-own-key.

Supported AI tools, exact setup steps, and the security model live in `llms-integration.txt`; the `tiddly` CLI that wires it all up is documented in `llms-cli-instructions.txt`. The current authoritative list of supported tools is the AI Integration settings page (linked below).

## Pricing

Three tiers — Free, Standard, and Pro — where higher tiers raise capacity (item counts, content length, version-history retention, rate limits) and Pro unlocks the in-app AI features. **tiddly.me is currently in beta, and all accounts have Pro access at no charge.** Authoritative limits and current prices: https://tiddly.me/pricing.

## Platform and Privacy

- **Self-hostable and open source** — the codebase is public at https://github.com/shane-kercheval/tiddly and can be self-hosted with no tier limits and full data control.
- **Infrastructure** — FastAPI backend, React frontend, PostgreSQL (encrypted at rest), Redis. Hosted on Railway's Pro tier (SOC 2, DDoS protection, daily backups).
- **Auth** — Auth0.
- **Multi-tenant** — content is isolated per user; there is no sharing.
- **Privacy** — no end-to-end encryption (it would prevent full-text search); a database administrator could technically access data. Self-host for complete privacy.

## Use Cases

- **Personal knowledge base** — bookmark articles (full text searchable), write notes, tag and filter into views like "all Python resources."
- **AI prompt library** — build reusable templates (e.g. a code-review prompt with `language`/`code`/`focus_area` arguments) and render them from any AI assistant over MCP.
- **Research and reference** — bookmark papers, link related items via relationships, track how notes evolve through version history.
- **AI-assisted content management** — let an assistant search, create, and edit your content and render prompts through natural conversation.

## Agent Resources

Start here, then follow the file that matches what the user wants to do:

- **Using the app** → `https://tiddly.me/llms-app-usage.txt` — how to organize, search, filter, edit, and the known issues to flag.
- **Connecting to AI tooling** → `https://tiddly.me/llms-integration.txt` — MCP setup, supported tools, Agent Skills, REST API, and the auth/security model.
- **The `tiddly` CLI** → `https://tiddly.me/llms-cli-instructions.txt` — full command reference and workflows (an agent already using the CLI can also run `tiddly ai-instructions`).

Human-facing references:

- Docs: https://tiddly.me/docs · Tips: https://tiddly.me/docs/tips
- Pricing (authoritative tier limits): https://tiddly.me/pricing
- AI Integration settings (authoritative supported-tools list): https://tiddly.me/app/settings/ai-integration
